items.title: Meta Pauses Work With Mercor After Data Breach Puts AI Industry Secrets at Risk
items.summary: Major AI labs are investigating a security incident that impacted Mercor, a leading data vendor. The incident could have exposed key data about how they train AI models.

items.title: Scientists Have Made a French Fry Breakthrough
items.summary: Researchers have developed a method of making french fries that results in a healthier bite without sacrificing crispiness.

items.title: The anonymous social app that thinks it can work in Saudi Arabia
items.summary: When Fizz quietly debuted in Saudi Arabia, founder and CEO Teddy Solomon wasn’t expecting the app to catch on like it did.

items.title: Tesla’s Texas factory workforce reportedly shrunk 22% in 2025
items.summary: Tesla's headcount fell from 21,191 workers to 16,506 workers in 2025, according to a report, as it grappled with its second straight year of declining sales.

items.title: OpenAI executive shuffle includes new role for COO Brad Lightcap to lead ‘special projects’
items.summary: In addition to Lightcap's new role, OpenAI CMO Kate Rouch will be stepping away from the company to focus on cancer recovery, with a plan to return when her health allows.

items.title: How elite US teams tackle combat rescue missions
items.summary: Air force pararescue units who specialise in CSAR missions are among the most highly trained in the US military.

items.title: Artemis II crew take 'spectacular' image of Earth
items.summary: The snap was taken aboard the Orion capsule by its commander, Reid Wiseman, as the crew head towards the Moon.

items.title: Burkina Faso must 'forget' about democracy, military leader says
items.summary: Capt Ibrahim Traoré, who seized power in 2023, says democratic rule would not work for his country.

items.title: Blue Owl caps private credit funds redemptions at 5% after steep request levels
items.summary: Blue Owl attributed the higher-than-usual requests to "heightened market concerns around AI-related disruption to software companies."

items.title: Visa launches new AI tools to manage the charge dispute process
items.summary: Visa's new tools are part of a larger push by major banks and financial institutions to incorporate AI into their businesses.

items.title: Warren Buffett says Iran bomb would make nuclear disaster harder to avoid
items.summary: The Berkshire Hathaway chairman said the growing number of nuclear-armed states has fundamentally altered the global risk landscape.

items.title: Stocks Sink in Broad AI Rout Sparked by China's DeepSeek
items.summary: U.S. stocks were mostly lower, with the Nasdaq leading declines as makers of AI infrastructure suffered steep falls, many in the double digits. Nvidia was down 16%.

items.title: Comex Gold, Silver Settle Lower
items.summary: Gold settled 1.4% lower, down two of the past three sessions, and silver fell 2.5%, down three of the past four sessions.

items.title: DeepSeek Won't Sink U.S. AI Titans
items.summary: Panic fueling the selloff of Nvidia, Broadcom and other tech giants is overblown.

items.summary: Acting Attorney General Todd Blanche on Thursday said he did not know if convicted sex offender Jeffrey Epstein spied for anybody before adding that there was no evidence in the Epstein files that the disgraced financier was a spy. Fox News’s Jesse Watters, the host of “Jesse Watters Primetime,” asked Blanche who “was Epstein spying…

items.summary: Two Senate Democrats are seeking answers from the Treasury Department about a plan to add President Trump’s signature to newly minted U.S. paper currency. Sens. items.title: Tillis sets Jan. 6 red line on attorney general nominee
items.summary: Sen. Thom Tillis (R-N.C.) said Thursday he will not support any nominee for attorney general if the candidate makes excuses for the Jan. 6 Capitol riot. President Trump dismissed Pam Bondi from the attorney general post before Tillis’s comments, which is expected to set up a confirmation battle on her successor later this year. “For…

items.title: Deafness reversed: One injection restores hearing in just weeks
items.summary: A new gene therapy is giving people born deaf the chance to hear, often within just weeks. In a small but groundbreaking study, researchers delivered a working copy of a key hearing gene directly into the inner ear using a single injection. All ten patients, ranging from young children to adults, experienced improved hearing, with some showing rapid gains in just one month.

items.title: Scientists discover why flu and COVID hit older adults so hard
items.summary: A new study reveals that aging lungs may play a major role in why flu and COVID can become so dangerous for older adults. Researchers found that certain lung cells can trigger an exaggerated immune response, creating clusters of inflammatory cells that end up damaging lung tissue instead of protecting it. In experiments, activating this aging-related signal in young mice caused their lungs to behave like older ones, leading to severe illness.

items.title: This tiny claw in a 500-million-year-old fossil just rewrote the origin of spiders
items.summary: What started as routine fossil cleaning turned into a major scientific surprise when researchers uncovered a tiny claw in a 500-million-year-old specimen where no claw should exist. That detail revealed Megachelicerax cousteaui, the oldest known relative of spiders, pushing the origins of this group back by 20 million years. The fossil shows that key features of modern spiders and horseshoe crabs were already emerging during the Cambrian Explosion.

items.title: When our minds wander to the body, it may affect mental health
items.summary: People’s minds sometimes wander to their bodily sensations, which may reduce symptoms of depression and ADHD, a new study suggests.

items.title: Supreme Court ruling on ‘conversion therapy’ puts medical talk in the hot seat
items.summary: In Chiles v. Salazar, the court ruled that a therapist has First Amendment protections. That could impact how talk therapy is regulated.

items.title: Huge Numbers tackles mathematics at its most incomprehensibly large
items.summary: Mathematician Richard Elwes surveys googology, the study of enormous numbers, in a new book.
